metadata.title: Build a Student AI Policy Classifier with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The four-mode matrix clarified why an institution-level ban collides with assignment-level reality. Take my course's assignment list and (1) classify each as Prohibited, Permitted with Disclosure, Required for Process, or Reflective Use, with the reasoning skill each one protects; (2) write the syllabus-ready policy paragraph for each mode; (3) take one Prohibited assignment and redesign it into Required for Process, telling me what changes in the task, what changes in the policy statement, and what the student now documents.

props.command: The four-mode matrix clarified why an institution-level ban collides with assignment-level reality. Take my course's assignment list and (1) classify each as Prohibited, Permitted with Disclosure, Required for Process, or Reflective Use, with the reasoning skill each one protects; (2) write the syllabus-ready policy paragraph for each mode; (3) take one Prohibited assignment and redesign it into Required for Process, telling me what changes in the task, what changes in the policy statement, and what the student now documents.
props.segment: Build a Student AI Policy Classifier

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. Build it with Claude — then take it apart.

Most AI use policies are written at the institution level but enforced at the assignment level — and the mismatch between them is where unintended violations happen. A blanket 'no AI' policy applied to an assignment that should require AI use for process documentation is a design failure, not a student failure.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: problem hook. Institution-level policy vs. assignment-level reality. The mismatch as a design gap, not a student behavior problem.

beats.narration_text: Pipe the assignment descriptions to Claude. For each assignment, classify the appropriate AI use policy mode: Prohibited, Permitted with Disclosure, Required for Process, or Reflective Use. For each, generate a one-paragraph syllabus-ready policy statement.

props.command: claude "For each assignment, classify the appropriate AI use policy mode: (1) Prohibited — AI use undermines the assignment's purpose, (2) Permitted with Disclosure — AI use is allowed if students document it, (3) Required for Process — students must use AI as part of the learning task, (4) Reflective Use — students use AI and then reflect on what it did and didn't do well. For each, provide a one-paragraph syllabus-ready policy statement." < assignments.txt

beats.narration_text: Read the script. It reads assignment descriptions from a file, sends each to Claude with the four-mode classification instruction, parses the mode and policy statement for each assignment, and outputs a formatted policy matrix with the classification and syllabus language side by side.

props.code:
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

MODES = [
    "Prohibited",
    "Permitted with Disclosure",
    "Required for Process",
    "Reflective Use",
]

def classify_assignments(assignments_file):
    assignments = Path(assignments_file).read_text()
    instruction = (
        "For each assignment, classify AI policy mode:\n"
        "Prohibited / Permitted with Disclosure /\n"
        "Required for Process / Reflective Use.\n"
        "Then write a syllabus-ready paragraph for each."
    )
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["claude", instruction],
        input=assignments, capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    print(result.stdout)

beats.narration_text: The classification appears. A debugging assignment designed to build debugging skill: Prohibited — the reasoning skill is what the assignment is testing. A literature review with AI-sourced summaries: Permitted with Disclosure — the synthesis is student-performed. A prompt engineering exercise: Required for Process — AI use is the learning task. An AI feedback review: Reflective Use — the student's reflection on Claude's limits is the evidence of learning.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: four-mode policy matrix. Each of four assignments placed in its mode with a brief rationale. Syllabus language excerpt below each.

beats.narration_text: The change: take one assignment classified as Prohibited and redesign it as Required for Process. What changes in the assignment design, what changes in the policy statement, and what the student does differently.

props.command: claude "Take this Prohibited assignment and redesign it as Required for Process — where AI use is a required part of the learning task. Show: (1) what changes in the assignment design, (2) the new syllabus policy statement, (3) what the student does differently in the redesigned version." < prohibited_assignment.txt

beats.narration_text: The redesigned assignment is still about debugging — but now the student must use Claude to diagnose the bug, document the prompts they used and the suggestions Claude made, evaluate whether the suggestion was correct, and explain why or why not. The policy statement changes from 'AI use is prohibited' to 'You are required to use an AI assistant and document every suggestion it makes and your evaluation of each.' The same skill — debugging — now includes the meta-skill of evaluating AI suggestions.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: before/after. Prohibited version: 'Find and fix the bug.' Required-for-Process version: 'Use Claude to diagnose, document suggestions, evaluate each one.' Policy language shown for each.

beats.narration_text: Policy mode is a design choice, not just a rule. The same topic can legitimately sit in any of the four quadrants depending on what the learning objective is and how the AI use is structured. The classifier makes the design decision explicit — and gives you syllabus language that's specific enough to answer the most common student edge-case question.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: teardown summary. Four modes as a design matrix. 'Same topic, four legitimate policies — depending on the objective.' The design choice, not the rule.

beats.narration_text: Next: Research What Makes AI Feedback Effective — an evidence review comparing AI feedback vs. human feedback across writing, mathematics, and programming.

The same topic can legitimately sit in any of the four quadrants depending on what the learning objective is and how the AI use is structured. The classifier makes the design decision explicit — and gives you syllabus language that's specific enough to answer the most common student edge-case question.
